reasoning: The Supreme Court held second oral arguments in Louisiana v. Callais on October 15, 2025. This case centers on whether Louisiana's SB8 map (featuring two majority-Black congressional districts) is an unconstitutional racial gerrymander. The lower court already struck down the map, and the Supreme Court signaled deep skepticism toward it by ordering rare reargument concerning whether Section 2 of the Voting Rights Act permits or requires such race-conscious districting under the 14th and 15th Amendments. During the October 2025 arguments, conservative Justices (particularly Thomas, Alito, Gorsuch, and Kavanaugh) exhibited hostility toward the race-conscious remedy and suggested imposing temporal limits on the VRA. Furthermore, the State of Louisiana itself supports striking down the map. Given the 6-3 conservative supermajority, the tone of oral arguments, the fact that striking down the map merely requires affirming the lower court's finding of a lack of narrow tailoring, and expert consensus that the Court seems "ready to curtail [a] major provision of the Voting Rights Act," it is highly likely (around 87%) that the Court will hold the map invalid before the end of the 2025-2026 term in June 2026.
